places.name: Alto Paraiso De Goias

visits.text: Chapada dos Veadeiros National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2001, protects 240,000 hectares of highland cerrado on a plateau 1.8 billion years old. Quartzite formations, the canyons of the Rio Preto and waterfalls of 80 and 120 meters create a landscape that feels outside of time.

The Vale da Lua (Valley of the Moon), 9 km from São Jorge, is a chaos of rocks sculpted by the Rio São Miguel into a lunar landscape. Craters, natural pools and rounded grey quartz formations bear witness to millions of years of erosion.

The Almécegas Waterfalls, on Fazenda São Bento near Alto Paraíso, offer two distinct experiences: Almécegas I, a 50-meter cascade with a dizzying viewpoint and a permanent rainbow; Almécegas II, a shorter drop with a deep pool perfect for swimming.

The Vila de São Jorge, gateway to the national park, is a village of 800 people with dirt streets, colorful houses, restaurants serving Goiás cuisine (arroz com pequi, empadão goiano) and crafts made from cerrado crystal stones.

activities.text: Hike to the Mirante da Janela, a natural rock window framing the view of the Rio Preto waterfalls. The 8 km round-trip trail crosses rugged cerrado with sections of rock scrambling over quartzite slabs.
Swim in the crystal waters of the Catarata dos Couros, a waterfall complex cascading down a canyon with over 300 meters of elevation change. The most spectacular fall plunges 100 meters.
Stargazing from Alto Paraíso. The low light pollution of the Chapada makes it one of Brazil's best spots for astronomy. The quartz-rich soil has given the area a mystical reputation.

places.name: Angra Dos Reis

visits.text: Angra dos Reis Bay is dotted with 365 islands—according to legend, one for each day of the year. This colonial port founded in 1502 by navigator Gonçalo Coelho was one of the most active on the Brazilian coast for trade routes between Europe and South America.

Igreja Nossa Senhora da Lapa da Boa Morte (1632) and Igreja Nossa Senhora do Carmo (1758) dot Angra's historic center. Praça Nilo Peçanha and its old buildings recall the era when the city served as a stopover for caravels restocking fresh water before the great crossing.

Ilha da Gipóia, the archipelago's second island, harbors beaches popular with families and a festive atmosphere around the floating bars of Praia do Dentista. Further south, Praia das Flechas is calmer, ideal for swimming.

Angra dos Reis Bay harbors one of Brazil's largest concentrations of shipwrecks, making its seabeds an exceptional dive site. Jorge Grego and Angra's Lagoa Azul dive spots allow you to observe multicolored fish, corals, and wrecks side by side.

activities.text: The ilhatour—a day-long boat excursion through the archipelago—is Angra dos Reis' signature activity. Swimming stops in the natural pools of Angra's Lagoa Azul (distinct from Ilha Grande's namesake site, deeper and flanked by cliffs), snorkeling on reefs, lunch stop on a white-sand beach, tour of private islands and their villas.

Hike to Banqueta waterfalls in Angra's mainland Atlantic forest: Véu de Noiva and Borboleta Azul falls succeed each other in a corridor of dense vegetation. Natural pools allow swimming along the way.

Hang-gliding flight over the bay: from Angra's heights, an aerial view of the island chain, the forest plunging into the sea, and Ilha Grande in the background reveals this archipelago's extraordinary scale.

visits.text: The Orla de Atalaia, a 6 km seafront promenade, is the beating heart of Aracaju. Cycling paths, monuments, sports courts and seafood restaurants line up between coconut and palm trees. Natural reef pools form at low tide.
The Mercado Municipal Antônio Franco, in the historic center, occupies a 1926 Art Nouveau building. Spices, renda lacework, artisanal cachaça from Sergipe and coalho cheese grilled on wooden skewers.
The Passarela do Caranguejo (Crab Walk), in Atalaia, lines up bars and restaurants specializing in crab with ice-cold beer. In the evening, the street turns into a party with live music and forró.

activities.text: Catamaran excursion on the Rio Sergipe to the Croa do Goré, a sandbar that emerges in the estuary. Lunch with your feet in the water, surrounded by mangroves and fishing boats.
Bike ride along the Orla de Atalaia at sunset. Kiosks serve coconut water and shrimp pastéis facing the ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

visits.text: Board a fast lancha in Barreirinhas for a downriver journey along the Rio Preguiças. The river winds between sandy banks and buriti palm groves before reaching the Pequenos Lençóis of Vassouras, the first dunes accessible by water.

The Farol de Mandacaru lighthouse rises 35 meters high on a sandy promontory. Climb its 160 steps: from the top, your gaze sweeps across the Rio Preguiças delta, the Atlantic Ocean, and the white expanse of Lençóis Maranhenses stretching to the horizon.

Caburé occupies a spectacular tongue of sand between the Preguiças River and the ocean. On one side, the calm, warm river waters; on the other, Atlantic breakers. Hammocks strung between palm trees invite a post-lunch siesta.

Atins, a village of 2,000 nestled among dunes, river, and ocean, retains its sandy lanes and low fishermen's houses. The atmosphere recalls what Jericoacoara was like twenty years ago: no paved roads, no crowds—just wind and silence.

Lençóis Maranhenses National Park unfolds across 155,000 hectares of white sand dunes as far as the eye can see. During the rainy season (January-June), valleys between dunes fill with turquoise freshwater lagoons, trapped by impermeable rock below. The Ponta do Mangue circuit leads to Lagoa Tropical and Lagoa Esmeralda, among the park's most crystalline pools.

The Canto de Atins circuit crosses an almost lunar landscape: wind-sculpted sand crests, crystal-clear pools where small fish swim, and not a tree in sight. The barefoot walk on warm sand between lagoons takes about two hours.

Atins beach stretches out facing the constant trade winds that make it a renowned kitesurfing spot. Sandbars sculpted by the Rio Preguiças form a natural lagoon sheltered from ocean swells—ideal for beginners.

activities.text: In late afternoon, head to the banks of the Rio Preguiças to watch guarás (scarlet ibis) flying over the river at twilight, crossing from shore to shore in crimson formations above the mangroves.

Try the famous grilled shrimp with molho secreto at Canto de Atins, the signature dish of the Lençóis Maranhenses region. The two restaurants at the point serve fresh catch of the day and carne de sol on terraces facing the dunes.
Climb the highest dune accessible from Atins for sunset. The white sand turns pink, then orange, while the lagoons below reflect the sky like mirrors.
Guided hike through the dunes and lagoons from Atins. A local guide knows the least crowded lagoons and adapts the route to the season's water level.
After the hike, relax in a hammock by the beach. Sand bars serve cajá caipirinhas and fresh coconut water facing the Atlantic.

places.name: Barra Do Garcas

visits.text: The Mirante do Cristo Rei overlooks Barra do Garças from atop the Serra do Roncador. The Christ statue — a modest cousin of Rio's — commands a 360° panorama of the confluence of the Rio Araguaia and the Rio Garças, the plains and the town below.

The Rio Araguaia, one of the great rivers of the cerrado, reveals white sand beaches (praias fluviais) from June to September when the water level drops. These sandbars in the middle of the river become swimming and camping spots.
The Parque Estadual da Serra Azul protects a patch of highland cerrado with natural hot springs, hiking trails and natural pools at a constant 38°C.

activities.text: Swimming and lounging on the river beaches of the Rio Araguaia. During the dry season, local boats shuttle between the sandbars that emerge mid-stream. Warm, gentle freshwater with no strong current.
Sunset on the banks of the Araguaia: the central-western Brazilian sky blazes with color above the river, with silhouettes of buriti palms in the foreground.

visits.text: The Rio Sucuri winds between dense forest banks. Its spring-fed water is so transparent you can see every detail on the riverbed: emerald-green algae, giant snails and piraputanga fish with their bright red fins. The snorkeling float takes about 40 minutes.

The Gruta do Lago Azul, a natural heritage site, descends 90 meters underground via a stairway carved into the rock. At the bottom, an intensely blue lake fills the main chamber. In December and January, a shaft of sunlight enters the cave mouth and lights up the water with an otherworldly glow.

The Aquário Natural starts with a trail through cerrado forest before opening onto a spring-fed pool where hundreds of fish glide among aquatic plants and submerged tree trunks. The crystal-clear water earns this place its name.

The Abismo Anhumas is a 72-meter-deep sinkhole accessible only by rappelling down. At the bottom, a royal-blue underground lake shelters giant stalagmites up to 19 meters tall. Scuba diving or snorkeling inside this limestone cathedral.

activities.text: Hiking and waterfalls at Parque das Cachoeiras or Boca da Onça: marked trails follow the Salobra River through the forest, linking several waterfalls — some plunging 150 meters. Swim in the natural pools between each cascade.
Wind down with a swim at the Balneário Municipal de Bonito, a natural pool fed by the Rio Formoso. Local families gather here for weekend barbecues.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

visits.text: The Esplanade of the Ministries lines up 17 identical buildings designed by Oscar Niemeyer on either side of a 2 km monumental axis. At the far end, the National Congress with its two domes — one inverted, one upright — has become the icon of Brasília.

The Metropolitan Cathedral, designed by Niemeyer in 1958, is a concrete hyperboloid whose 16 curved pillars support a ceiling of blue and green stained glass. You enter through a dark underground tunnel before emerging into a flood of light.

The Praça dos Três Poderes unites the Palácio do Planalto (presidency), the Supreme Court and the Congress around an austere esplanade. The Panteão da Pátria and the Museu da Cidade complete this ensemble listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987.

The Museu Nacional, a white dome 40 meters across planted like a saucer on the esplanade, was Niemeyer's last major work, inaugurated for his 99th birthday. Temporary exhibitions on art and architecture.

activities.text: UNESCO architecture loop by car or bicycle: link the Palácio da Alvorada (presidential residence with marble columns), the Dom Bosco Chapel (interior bathed in blue light from 12 tons of stained glass), and the JK Memorial, tomb of the man who built Brasília.
Sunset from the Ponte JK, whose three steel arches span Lake Paranoá. Golden reflections on the water and the silhouette of government buildings make for a spectacular panorama.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

places.name: Campo Grande

visits.text: The Parque das Nações Indígenas, a sprawling 119-hectare green space in the city center, holds a lake, shaded trails and the Museu das Culturas Dom Bosco. Capybaras, herons and toucans populate the banks. The museum houses an outstanding collection of artifacts from the indigenous peoples of Mato Grosso do Sul.

The Mercadão Municipal of Campo Grande gathers the flavors of central-western Brazil: Japanese sobá noodles brought by immigrant communities, dried meats from the Pantanal, artisanal cheeses, doce de leite and cerrado fruits.

Rua 14 de Julho, the pedestrian artery downtown, is lined with shops, cafés and Art Deco buildings dating from the city's economic boom of the 1930s–1950s. The Morada dos Baís, a 1913 mansion, now hosts exhibitions and concerts.

The Museu José Antônio Pereira tells the story of Campo Grande's founding in 1899. The reconstructed wooden house, surrounded by cerrado gardens, recalls the life of the first settlers who came from Minas Gerais.

activities.text: Detour to the southern Pantanal: from Campo Grande, road MS-262 dips into the wetlands in under an hour. Lookout points along the Estrada Parque let you spot caimans, capybaras, jabiru storks and macaws from the car.

places.name: Canoa Quebrada

visits.text: Canoa Quebrada's cliffs rise 30 meters of reddish walls above an emerald ocean. The moon-and-star symbol, carved into the sandstone by a local artist in the 1970s, marks the site's entrance from atop the dunes.

Aracati's historic center, 13 km inland, preserves 275 buildings classified by IPHAN. Portuguese-tiled sobrados from the 18th century line Rua Grande, remnants of when the river port exported charque (dried beef) across Brazil.

The Museu Jaguaribano, housed in the Solar do Barão de Aracati, chronicles the charque cycle and colonial prosperity. The Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, an 18th-century temple, dominates the main square.
In nearby Majorlândia village, artisans create garrafinhas de areia colorida: miniature landscapes composed of differently colored sands poured into glass bottles. A unique craft born on this coast.

activities.text: Buggy tour along the cliffs and dunes, passing shrimp farms and lagoons. The zipline descent from the cliff summit offers an aerial view of red rock meeting turquoise water.
Sunset from Canoa Quebrada's Duna do Pôr do Sol. The ritual mirrors Jeri: climb up, settle in, and watch the sun set the red cliffs ablaze before plunging into the Atlantic.
Evening on Rua Broadway (officially Rua Dragão do Mar), Canoa's nightlife heart. Reggae, forró, and electronic music blend in bars and restaurants that awaken at sundown.
Sail out with local fishermen in a jangada beyond the cliffs. These traditional flat-bottomed sailboats glide across turquoise waters—an ancestral navigation facing a postcard landscape.

places.name: Chapada Dos Guimaraes Pousada Do Parque

visits.text: The center of Chapada dos Guimarães is organized around the Igreja de Sant'Ana, founded in 1779 and protected by IPHAN. Shops sell semi-precious stones and cerrado wood crafts. Perched at 800 meters altitude, the village enjoys cooler temperatures compared to the Cuiabá plains.

Cachoeira Véu de Noiva plunges 86 meters from a reddish sandstone amphitheater. A 650-meter trail leads to the viewpoint overlooking the Coxipózinho stream valley. Red-and-green macaws nest in cliff crevices. Below, brachiopod fossils embedded in the rock testify to a 400-million-year-old marine past.

Cidade de Pedra, atop the national park cliffs, features sandstone pillars sculpted by erosion into silhouettes resembling skyscrapers. From the viewpoint, your gaze plunges into canyons, veredas (buriti palm corridors), and river springs winding through the cerrado 300 meters below.

The Circuito das Cachoeiras links seven waterfalls over 8 km of trail through cerrado and gallery forest: Andorinhas, Prainha, Degrau, Pulo, and Sete de Setembro. Along the way, Casa de Pedra—a massive water-carved cave—opens natural chambers in the rock.

activities.text: Drive to Mirante Alto do Céu, 8 km from the village, for sunset. The sandstone cliffs turn blood-red as the sun plunges behind the plateau. King vultures circle overhead in the updrafts.
Birdwatching at dawn in the national park's cerrado. Red macaws, king vultures, orange-billed toucans, and swifts share the cliffs and canopy. An accredited guide is mandatory for most trails.
Swim in the natural pools of Cachoeirinha and Cachoeira dos Namorados, accessible on foot from the Véu de Noiva parking area. The cool water contrasts sharply with the cerrado heat.

visits.text: Cristalino Lodge nestles on a meander of the Rio Cristalino, in the heart of an 11,399-hectare private reserve of primary Amazonian rainforest. Accessible only by boat after an hour's drive from Alta Floresta, it protects six distinct ecosystems. Over 596 bird species have been recorded here.
The Ficus Trail crosses terra firme forest to a giant fig tree whose buttress roots rise several meters high. Brown capuchin monkeys and red-nosed sakis feed in the canopy above the path.

The 50-meter canopy tower, one of the tallest in the Americas, rises from the middle of the reserve. From the top, the forest stretches as far as the eye can see—an ocean of green rippling in the wind. Hawks, macaws, toucans, and colorful cotingas cross your field of vision. At sunrise, mist tears open over the canopy.

The Saleiro House, a wooden cabin perched in the reserve, overlooks a saladeiro—soil naturally rich in minerals. Tapirs, collared peccaries, giant anteaters, and tamanduás come to feed. The silent watch can last two hours before a rustle in the leaves betrays a visitor.

The Taboca Trail follows a seasonally flooded forest zone. White-whiskered spider monkeys—endemic species and emblem of the neighboring state park—travel in troops through the canopy. Bromeliads and heliconias carpet the damp understory.

The Sierra Trail crosses hilly terrain covered with semi-deciduous forest. Wild orchids cling to branches; scarlet bromeliads punctuate the understory. From rocky outcrops, the view plunges into the valley and canopy below.
The Cacao Trail follows an area where native cacao trees grow wild. Birds feed on the split pods. Anteaters, armadillos, and capuchin monkeys frequent this section, more open than the others.

activities.text: Evening boat trip on the Rio Cristalino. Caimans surface, capybaras graze on the banks, and tiger herons stand motionless. Guides spot wildlife with flashlights in the gathering darkness.
Swim from the lodge's floating dock, anchored in the crystal-clear waters of the Rio Cristalino. The rapids upstream offer an invigorating dip after forest hikes.

Dawn canoe on the Rio Cristalino. Paddles glide silently over translucent water as capped herons, kingfishers, and anhingas dry their wings on low branches. With luck, a giant otter crosses the current ahead of your bow.

Boat excursion to the confluence of the Rio Teles Pires and Rio Cristalino. The dark waters of the Teles Pires meet the clear waters of the Cristalino. Parrots, parakeets, and storks nest on the neighboring island. The sunset here is spectacular.

Second climb to the canopy tower, this time at dawn. The forest awakens in a concert of calls: the metallic song of the bellbird, blue macaws crossing the sky in pairs, multicolored-billed toucans emerging from branches. Low-angle light transforms mist into gold.

Picnic on a sandbank of the Rio Cristalino, followed by a swim in the rapids. Guides prepare lunch in the middle of the forest while blue morpho butterflies, electric-blue wings flashing, flutter between the banks.

places.name: Cuiaba

visits.text: The Cachoeira Véu de Noiva, postcard image of Chapada dos Guimarães, plunges 86 meters from the rim of a red-orange sandstone amphitheater. Scarlet macaws nest in the cracks of the cliff face. The viewpoint, a gentle 550-meter walk from the parking lot, gives a bird's-eye view of the falls.

Chapada dos Guimarães National Park protects 33,000 hectares of highland cerrado. The Circuito das Cachoeiras strings together seven waterfalls over 6 km: Sorrisal, Pulo, Prainha and the spectacular Andorinhas, where swallows dive through the curtain of water at dusk.

The Mirante Geodésico, the highest point of the Chapada at 845 meters, marks the geodetic center of South America. The view sweeps across sandstone escarpments, cerrado forest and, on a clear day, the plains of Cuiabá on the horizon.
The town center of Chapada dos Guimarães revolves around a tree-shaded square and the Igreja de Sant'Ana, founded in 1811 and listed by IPHAN. Shops sell semi-precious stones and cerrado-wood crafts.

activities.text: Swim in the natural rock pools of Cachoeirinha and Cachoeira dos Namorados, both a short walk from the Véu de Noiva car park. The cool water is a welcome contrast to the cerrado heat.
Catch the sunset from Mirante Alto do Céu, 8 km from the center of the Chapada. The cliffs shift from rust to gold as the sun sinks behind the plateau.

places.name: Cunha

visits.text: Cunha emerges from the mists of Serra da Bocaina at 850 meters elevation. Founded as Vila do Falcão in 1730 on the old gold route linking the mines of Minas Gerais to the port of Paraty, the town retains its character as a preserved Brazilian rural village deep in the Atlantic forest.

Casa do Artesão brings together local ceramicists around a collection of handcrafted pieces. Since the 1970s, Cunha has become Brazil's ceramics capital: a Pottery Route crosses the countryside, dotted with workshops where artisans still use the Japanese noborigama kiln technique, whose free flames create unpredictable color effects.

Parque Estadual da Serra do Mar - Núcleo Cunha protects one of the last remnants of cloud forest in Brazil, above 1,000 meters elevation. Its trails traverse century-old trees, springs, and waterfalls including Cachoeira do Barracão, nestled in a cirque of rocks and natural pools.
Cunha's lavender farm astonishes: rows of lavender, rosemary, and geranium spread beneath a Provençal-style house lost in the Brazilian hills. Artisan products sold on-site.

activities.text: Hike to Pedra da Macela (1,840 m): 2.3 km climb through forest, with 360° panoramic views over Serra da Bocaina, the coast of Paraty, and Ilha Grande in the distance. The border between São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro States stretches beneath your feet.
Visit ceramics workshops along the rural road. Rental shops offer bikes to tour the Pottery Route and stop at active noborigama kilns, where wood-firing produces unique pieces with each firing.

Swim at Cachoeira do Pimenta: a waterfall accessible on foot from Cunha's rural zone, surrounded by dense Atlantic forest. Natural pools carved by the current offer welcome refreshment after the hike.

visits.text: The route from Tutóia crosses the Piauí-Ceará border, passing through Chaval and Barroquinha. The landscape evolves from coastal plains to low cliffs and coconut groves of Ceará's far west.

Praia das Curimãs opens onto a vast bay bordered by dunes and mangroves. Morning brings calm seas; afternoon trade winds whip up powerful waves. Fishermen's colorful jangadas strain at their anchors along the strand.
Curimãs is an authentic fishing community with virtually no tourist infrastructure. Low houses line sandy lanes, nets dry on the beach, and children play football at low tide.
Head to neighboring Praia de Bitupitá, accessible by beach or track: a wide band of fine sand, vegetated dunes, and a few artisanal fishing boats.

Spend a morning moving to the village rhythm: Curimãs fishermen depart at dawn and return between 9 and 10 a.m. On the beach, fresh fish is traded right on the sand in a lively, good-natured atmosphere.
Explore the freshwater lagoons that form behind Curimãs dunes at low tide. These natural pools, ringed by restinga vegetation, offer calm swimming sheltered from the wind.

Excursion by 4x4 or buggy to Camocim along the beach (about 40 min). En route, the coast alternates between low red clay cliffs, deserted beaches, and mangrove estuaries where fishing canoes are moored.

In Camocim, Barra dos Remédios forms a tongue of sand between river and sea, reminiscent of Caburé. Jangadas are moored along the bank, and Ilha do Amor, accessible on foot at low tide, offers an isolated sandbar in the middle of the estuary.

activities.text: Sail out with a local fisherman on a jangada for a one- to two-hour sea voyage. These traditional flat-bottomed sailboats glide across turquoise waters, cruising the coast from dune to dune.
Explore by 4x4 or on foot along the coastline between Curimãs and Bitupitá: isolated coves among mangroves, lagoons formed at low tide, and ocean panoramas from the dunes.
Go tidepooling at low tide on the exposed sandbars. Locals gather shellfish, crabs, and small fish trapped in pools among rocks and mangrove roots.
Kitesurfing session or beginner lessons on Curimãs beach. Consistent afternoon trade winds (15-25 knots) and the flat water between sandbars make this a favorite spot for practitioners.
Watch fishermen return and prepare grilled fish on the beach in late afternoon. Lobsters, mackerel, and red mullet are cooked over coconut shell coals, served with farofa and lime.
Sunset from the Curimãs dunes: the sky shifts from gold to fiery red as jangada silhouettes cut across the horizon.

places.name: Curitiba

visits.text: Curitiba's Botanical Garden, opened in 1991, fans out in geometric French-style beds in front of an Art Nouveau greenhouse of glass and steel inspired by London's Crystal Palace. The scientific herbarium holds over 330,000 specimens.

The Oscar Niemeyer Museum, nicknamed 'The Eye' for its giant eye-shaped tower, houses rotating exhibitions of Brazilian and international contemporary art. The sprawling sculpture garden surrounding it is a stroll in itself.

Largo da Ordem, Curitiba's colonial heart dating back to the 17th century, lines up colorful townhouses, the Igreja da Ordem Terceira de São Francisco (1737) and the Curitiba Memorial. On Sundays, the Feira de Artesanato fills the cobblestones with hundreds of artisan stalls.

Praça Tiradentes, birthplace of the city founded in 1693, is dominated by the Catedral Basílica de Nossa Senhora da Luz dos Pinhais, built in 1893. A stone obelisk on the square commemorates the city's founding under the Portuguese Crown.

activities.text: Parque Tanguá, a former quarry converted in 1996, features a 65-meter artificial waterfall cascading down a sandstone cliff. Climb to the viewpoint for Curitiba's finest sunset: the city spreads out below all the way to the Serra do Mar.

The Ópera de Arame, a theater with a tubular steel frame and transparent polycarbonate roof, floats above a lake in a former quarry. A walkway over the mirror-like water leads to the entrance. Jazz and MPB concerts echo from the floating stage.

Try barreado, the signature dish of the Paraná coast: meat slow-cooked for hours in a sealed clay pot, served with manioc flour and banana. Restaurants on Largo da Ordem also serve Italian and Polish specialties inherited from waves of immigration.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

map_keywords: Largo da Ordem historic center Curitiba

visits.text: The Mercado Público of Florianópolis, a market hall from 1899 in the heart of the historic center, overflows with fresh fish, seafood, spices and artisanal products. The counters serve locally farmed oysters with a squeeze of lime.

Lagoa da Conceição, a 20 km² lagoon ringed by lush green hills, is the bohemian soul of the island. Settled by Azorean colonists in 1750, it still has the Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Conceição and a small center of lanes lined with bars and galleries.

The Joaquina Dunes, a vast sweep of sand between the lagoon and the ocean, are the birthplace of Brazilian sandboarding. From the top, you can see the surf of Joaquina on one side and the calm waters of the Lagoa on the other.

The Ponte Hercílio Luz, a 340-meter suspension bridge from 1926 — the longest in Brazil — offers sweeping views of the bay and the city center. Long closed to traffic, it has been restored as a pedestrian landmark and symbol of Florianópolis.

activities.text: Swimming and surfing at Praia de Joaquina, one of southern Brazil's most famous breaks. Powerful waves draw international competitions. For calmer water, Praia de Campeche offers white sand and natural tide pools at low tide.

Hike to Lagoinha do Leste, a secluded beach only accessible on foot from Praia do Matadeiro. The 3 km trail winds through Atlantic Forest and opens onto a crescent of sand framed by cliffs — one of the wildest landscapes on the island.

In the evening, grab a table at a terrace restaurant in Barra da Lagoa for a sequência de camarão — a succession of shrimp dishes: grilled, breaded, in coconut sauce and moqueca. Fishing boats pulled up on the sand complete the scene.

places.name: Fortaleza

visits.text: The Centro Dragão do Mar Arte e Cultura, in the Praia Iracema district, houses a planetarium, Ceará Museum of Contemporary Art, and Museum of Ceará Culture (featuring jangadas and forró). Open-air performance stages. Fortaleza's vibrant cultural heart.

The Teatro José de Alencar, inaugurated in 1910, is the Northeast's architectural jewel. Art Nouveau facade in wrought iron imported from Scotland, stained glass windows, gardens by Roberto Burle Marx. Guided tours are free.
The Ponte dos Ingleses (1921) extends into the sea from Praia Iracema. Its partially ruined metal structure has become a romantic viewpoint over the ocean, spectacular at sunset.
The Mercado Central houses 600 vendors across 4 floors: labyrinth-point lace, ceramics, hammocks, cashew nuts, nordestino crafts. One of Ceará's largest artisan markets.

activities.text: Praia do Futuro, 4 miles from downtown, lines up large barracas with lounge chairs, crab restaurants, pools, and live music. On Thursdays, the signature dish is crab in molho sauce.
The Feirinha Beira Mar sets up every evening at 6 pm along the coastal avenue: 800 stalls of jewelry, lace, clothing, and specialties. Musicians and forró dancers animate the aisles.
At dawn, Mucuripe's fish market comes alive by 5 am: jangadas—traditional sailboats—return from night fishing and unload tuna, lobsters, and tropical fish. Stalls grill fish on the spot.

places.name: Foz Do Iguacu

visits.text: The Itaipu Dam, built from 1975 to 1984 on the Paraná River between Brazil and Paraguay, is one of the largest hydroelectric plants in the world. Its 20 turbines generate 15% of Brazil's electricity and 90% of Paraguay's. The panoramic tour reveals the colossal scale of the structure.

Iguaçu Falls from the Brazilian side: 275 cascades spread across 2.7 km. A 1,200-meter walkway of catwalks delivers a sweeping panoramic view of the falls — 80% of which are on Argentine territory. UNESCO World Heritage since 1986.

Parque das Aves, just 100 meters from the national park entrance, is home to 1,300 birds of 130 species across 16 hectares of Atlantic Forest: toucans, parrots, cassowaries and macaws. Walk-through aviaries bring you remarkably close.
On the Argentine side, the upper and lower circuits of Iguazú National Park let you approach the falls both from above and from their base. Curtains of water frame the catwalks on every side.
The Garganta del Diablo (Devil's Throat) is reached via a catwalk above the river. A permanent cloud of mist engulfs the 80-meter-deep chasm — the most intense experience at Iguazú Falls.

activities.text: Arrive at the national park on the Brazilian side at dawn, right when the gates open at 7am, before the tour groups flood in. The first rays of sunlight strike the cascades and paint rainbows in the spray.
At the Marco Três Fronteiras, where Argentina, Brazil and Paraguay meet at the confluence of the rivers, three obelisks in national colors face each other across the Paraná. The best spot for sunset.
The Macuco Safari: a zodiac speeds up the river from the Brazilian side straight to the base of the falls, then punches through a curtain of water. Bring a change of clothes — you will be drenched.

visits.text: Galinhos stretches across a sand peninsula 500 meters wide, squeezed between the ocean on one side and an estuary on the other. With 1,200 inhabitants, the village is accessible only by boat or 4×4 along the beach at low tide.
Galinhos salt flats raise enormous white mountains amid the mangrove. Salt is extracted directly from seawater—Rio Grande do Norte is Brazil's leading salt producer.
The village of Galos, even smaller than Galinhos, is reached on foot or by donkey cart along the estuary. Its sandy lanes lead to a beach where you pass from river to ocean in a few steps.

At the peninsula's tip, the Galinhos lighthouse dominates a circular beach battered by waves. Wind farm turbines turn slowly in the background, sculpting a futuristic silhouette against ancestral sand.
Duna do Capim emerges amid lagoons like a miniature Lençóis Maranhenses landscape. Freshwater lagoons form between dunes after rains—their hues oscillate from emerald green to deep blue.

Navigate the mangrove channels by motorboat. The boatman plunges his hand into muddy water and pulls out a peixe-morcego, a flat-bodied prehistoric fish that's immediately released—a fascinating local ritual.
The salt flat mountain rises in dazzling white flanks above the green mangrove. Capture the right angle and the scene evokes a snowy landscape beneath the tropics.
Blue lagoons between the dunes form natural pools of warm water. Immaculate sand descends gently toward crystalline water—a bath worthy of a Polynesian lagoon.

activities.text: Village discovery by donkey-drawn cart, the only local transport besides buggies. Sandy lanes run past colorful houses—welcome to a Brazil beyond time.
Observe the salt flats and mangroves from the estuary. The vegetation shelters crabs, oysters, white and blue herons—and sometimes seahorses in shallow waters.
Sunset from the peninsula's natural pools. Warm water rises to your knees, the sky blazes—a moment of absolute serenity.
Half-day boat excursion: departure from the village, mangrove crossing, stop at the salt flats, swimming in Duna do Capim lagoons, and grilled fish lunch at Galos.
Buggy ride across the dunes to the deserted beaches north of the peninsula. Sand crunches beneath the wheels, wind lifts white swirls—a desert bordered by the Atlantic.
Meet local fishermen returning in their boats. Taste fresh oysters served waterside with a squeeze of lime—Galinhos gastronomy is experienced barefoot in the sand.

places.name: Ilha Grande

visits.text: Vila do Abraão, Ilha Grande's only real village, opens onto a cove surrounded by jungle-covered peaks. No cars: dirt lanes, colorful pousadas, moored boats. The entire island has been a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 1992 and inscribed as a World Heritage Site in 2019.

Praia de Lopes Mendes stretches for 3 km of immaculate white sand, ranked among the world's most beautiful beaches. Count on 2 hours of walking round-trip from Abraão through Atlantic forest to reach it—the trail is part of the experience. Offshore winds and waves also make it the island's surf spot.

The Lazareto ruins testify to the island's troubled past: a 19th-century colonial lazaretto for sick passengers landing in Brazil, then a maximum-security prison closed in 1994. The ruins scattered in the vegetation are accessible on foot from Abraão.

Praia do Aventureiro, protected by an integral biological reserve, is one of the island's most remote beaches. Accessible only by boat or a 4-hour trail from Abraão, its calm waters, white sand, and forest plunging into the sea make it timeless.

Ilha Grande's Lagoa Azul—not to be confused with the namesake site in Angra dos Reis—is a natural pool formed between two rocks, with translucent blue waters, accessible by boat from Abraão in 20 minutes. One of the island's best snorkeling spots.

Saco do Céu—the Bay of Heaven—is an enclosed cove whose name comes from the sky's reflection in its perfectly calm waters. Surrounded by forested mountains and nearly closed upon itself, this bay is renowned for blazing sunsets.

Praia de Dois Rios is accessible on foot from Abraão via a 2-hour forest trail. This long isolated arc of sand borders the site of the former prison—foundations and some walls survive in the vegetation, recalling the island's decades of penitentiary isolation.

activities.text: Hike to Cachoeira da Feiticeira: 1h30 walk from Abraão through rainforest, crossing clearings and clear-water streams. The waterfall plunges 15 meters into a natural pool ringed by boulders and dense vegetation.
Kayak or paddle from Abraão: 20 minutes' paddling to reach Praia de Crena, where restaurants serve seafood caught that morning. The bay's interior waters stay calm all day.
Sunset from Pico do Abraão, a hill accessible in 30 minutes' walk from the village: panoramic view over the bay, forested peaks, and the lights of Vila do Abraão glowing at twilight.

Ascent of Pico do Papagaio (987 m): 4h30 round-trip from Abraão, 956 m elevation gain through jungle. Panoramic view over the entire island, Angra dos Reis Bay, and the coast all the way to Paraty. One of the Costa Verde's most spectacular treks.

Boat excursion around the island: stops at Ilha Grande's Lagoa Azul for snorkeling on coral reefs, swimming in the emerald waters of Saco do Céu, stops at deserted beaches. Sea turtles, multicolored fish, and dolphins offshore on good days.
Scuba diving from Abraão: Ilha Grande's seabeds shelter coral reefs, wrecks, and exceptional fauna. Jorge Grego and Lagoa Azul dive sites are accessible from the village in under 30 minutes by boat.

places.name: Itacare

visits.text: Itacaré, a former fishing village on the Cocoa Coast, sits at the mouth of the Rio de Contas, wrapped in Atlantic Forest. Its colorful streets, low houses and boats moored at the port recall the era when cacao made the region's fortune. The 'witch's broom' disease devastated the plantations in the 1980s, pushing the town toward surfing and ecotourism.

Praia da Tiririca is Bahia's best surf spot. Its powerful, consistent waves attract national competitions year-round. Beginners will find calmer water at Praia da Concha, a family-friendly beach with barracas and a panoramic viewpoint from Ponta do Xaréu.

The 4-beach trail cuts through Atlantic Forest from Engenhoca to Itacarezinho over 4 km. Each cove is a world apart: Engenhoca for surfing, Havaizinho for small reef-fringed inlets, near-deserted Camboinha under the coconut palms, and Itacarezinho, a 4 km arc of sand bordered by endless palms.

Prainha, reached only by a 40-minute hike through tropical forest from the center of Itacaré, is one of the most beautiful beaches in Bahia. The trail crosses dense canopy before opening onto a white-sand cove hemmed in by lush vegetation.

activities.text: Boat trip on the Rio de Contas: the river, born in the Chapada Diamantina, winds through mangroves and cacao plantations before emptying into the sea. Swimming stops at waterfalls accessible from the river, including the Cachoeira do Cleandro.
The Cachoeira do Tijuípe, 15 km south of Itacaré, tumbles through the Atlantic Forest. Its unusually warm water and wide pool make for a comfortable swim after the short access trail.
Beginner surf lesson at Praia da Tiririca or Praia de Resende. Local schools offer 2-hour classes with boards and wetsuits provided. Itacaré's waves are known for their year-round consistency.

places.name: Jericoacoara

visits.text: The 4×4 transfer from Fortaleza first crosses the inland plains of Ceará before threading through coastal dunes. The final hour plays out entirely on sand, weaving between mangroves and lagoons—the adventure begins long before arrival.

The village of Jericoacoara, a former fishing hamlet discovered in the 1980s, preserves its car-free sandy streets. The Washington Post once ranked it among the world's ten most beautiful beaches. Going barefoot here isn't optional—it's the only way to move through town.

Climb the Duna do Pôr do Sol, a massive golden dune just west of the village. From its crest, the Atlantic stretches endlessly while the sun ignites the horizon—this is Jericoacoara's nightly ritual, and everyone gathers here to witness it.

Pedra Furada rises 1.7 km from the village, a stone arch carved by waves at the foot of Serrote hill. In July, the sun sets precisely through the arch's opening—a phenomenon locals applaud every evening.

Lagoa do Paraíso unfolds across 15 km² of transparent freshwater surrounded by towering dunes. Hammocks suspended above the water invite contemplation. The contrast between turquoise lagoon and white dunes is breathtaking.
Ascend the Serrote, a small 95-meter serra that dominates the village. The lighthouse (farol) occupies its summit. The 360° view reveals the dune field, rocky coastline, and the ocean's immensity.

Lagoa Azul, more intimate than its larger sibling, nestles its blue-tinted waters between wind-sculpted dunes. Beach clubs set up hammocks and tables at the water's edge—lunch with your feet in a lagoon, a simple yet unforgettable luxury.

activities.text: Sunset from the summit of Duna do Pôr do Sol. Locals and travelers alike applaud each evening as the sun drops into the ocean—a ritual that sets the tone for your entire stay.
First dinner in the village: grilled seafood, caju caipirinha, and live forró in the sandy bars. The lanes come alive beneath the stars, barefoot on warm sand.
Launch into the dunes by buggy: Lagoa Azul, Lagoa do Paraíso, Tatajuba dunes, and river crossings. Wind whips the sand, the buggy plummets down steep slopes—adrenaline guaranteed.
Strap on harness and wing for a kitesurfing session at Praia do Preá, 17 km from the village. Constant trade winds and steady waves make this one of Brazil's premier spots.
Glide down the sand on a sandboard from the summit of Duna do Pôr do Sol. Rent a board from local vendors and ride the slope facing the ocean—pure freedom.
Horseback ride along the deserted beach at sunset. Riders trace the dunes and Rio Guriú mangroves, where seahorses sometimes appear in the shallow waters.

places.name: Maceio

visits.text: The natural pools of Pajuçara form at low tide 2 km offshore. Colorful fishing jangadas cross the emerald water in 15 minutes to reach the coral banks where waist-deep crystal water reveals tropical fish, starfish and corals — a natural aquarium.

Praia de Ponta Verde, Maceió's main urban beach, unfurls its white sand along a coconut-palm-lined promenade. Its reefs also form natural pools at low tide. The Farol de Ponta Verde, a small lighthouse at the end of the jetty, offers a panoramic view of the bay.

The historic Jaraguá district, Maceió's former commercial port, lines up restored warehouses, art galleries and the Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos (1867). Art Deco buildings on Rua Sá e Albuquerque stand beside colorful colonial facades.

The Feirinha de Artesanato de Pajuçara, a beachfront craft market, sells filé lacework (an Alagoas specialty), painted ceramics, embroidery and coconut products. Nearby barracas serve tapiocas and crab in sauce.

activities.text: Jangada trip to the Pajuçara natural pools: gliding over the corals reveals a multicolored seabed. The jangadeiro provides mask and snorkel, and floating bar-boats serve drinks and seafood right on the reef.
Sunset at Praia de Jatiúca, quieter than Pajuçara. Beach kiosks (barracas) serve local specialties: sururu (freshwater mussels), fried macaxeira and passion-fruit caipirinha.

map_keywords: Igreja Nossa Senhora Rosário Pretos Maceió

visits.text: The descent from Serra da Ibiapaba to the coast takes about four hours. The landscape transforms: high-altitude forest gives way to the dry plains of the sertão, then to coconut groves and wind farms along Ceará's western coast.

Icaraí de Amontada (Icaraizinho), a village of 5,000, occupies a 3.7-mile bay between dunes and a river mouth. Sandy and cobbled streets, low houses without fences, and jangadas pulled up on the beach give it an end-of-the-world feel.

Praia de Moitas, 5 miles from Icaraizinho, is an isolated beach with pale sand and morning calm seas. At low tide, sandbars emerge to form natural pools. The atmosphere is that of a preserved fishing village, virtually without tourist infrastructure.

The Nossa Senhora dos Navegantes chapel, at the heart of Icaraizinho, serves as the village landmark. Around it, a handful of shops and restaurants form a peaceful centrinho, far from the bustle of Fortaleza or Jericoacoara.

The Rio Aracatiaçu winds through dunes and mangroves before meeting the ocean at Moitas. From Barra das Moitas, board a small boat heading upriver: the water shifts from ochre to emerald green as you penetrate the mangrove.

The Túnel do Amor is the journey's highlight: mangroves form a canopy over the river. At high tide, the water takes on a translucent turquoise hue and the canoe glides silently through a corridor of green. Crabs scuttle across aerial roots.
Ilha das Ostras, a sandbar in the middle of the river, is an unmissable stop. Mangrove oysters are harvested and shucked on the spot. You'll eat them with your feet in the water, facing the dunes.

The tall dunes of Moitas, sculpted by trade winds, offer panoramas over the river mouth, ocean, and neighboring wind farm turbine towers. Fine sand is dotted with coconut palms whose roots have been exposed by erosion.

Excursion to Caetanos, a village even more isolated than Icaraizinho, 12 miles to the east. The buggy route crosses a wind farm and passes a century-old cemetery buried by dunes—a surreal image of white crosses amid golden sand.

The Lençóis Cearenses, a miniature version of Lençóis Maranhenses, hide behind the dunes of Caetanos. From May to September, freshwater lagoons fill between mounds of white sand, forming warm pools bordered by low vegetation.

Praia do Jiqui surprises with its coconut palms whose roots have been exposed by coastal erosion and its natural freshwater spring (bica) gushing from the sandy cliff. The contrast between the cool spring water and warm sea is striking.

Lagoa de Flexeiras, 4 miles from Icaraizinho, is a translucent freshwater lake bordered by coconut palms and dunes. Barely three feet deep, the warm, still water makes it an ideal swimming spot for the whole family.

activities.text: Take an exploratory walk on Icaraizinho beach at sunset. Constant trade winds fill the sails of kitesurfers while fishermen sort the day's catch on the sand. Wind turbine towers stand in silhouette against the reddening sky.
Dine on grilled fish or fresh seafood at one of the centrinho restaurants. Dishes are prepared from the morning catch: grouper, shrimp, octopus, served with farofa and lime.
Take your first swim in the natural lagoon that forms at low tide in front of Icaraizinho: warm, shallow water without waves—an ideal stretch to unwind after the long drive.
Buggy excursion from Icaraizinho to Moitas along the beach. The route passes giant wind turbine towers, crosses stretches of deserted beach, and stops at a swing suspended over the Rio Aracatiaçu.
Navigate the Túnel do Amor by boat at high tide. The contrast between the cool shade of the mangrove and the turquoise glow of the water is striking.
Sunset from the Duna do Pôr do Sol at Moitas: the sun plunges into the Atlantic while kite wings punctuate the sky with color.
Hike or buggy through the Lençóis Cearenses. Between lagoons, the barefoot walk on warm sand recalls the Lençóis Maranhenses experience, with more intimate landscapes and without the crowds.
Swim in Lagoa da Cobra, one of the area's largest lagoons, surrounded by wind-sculpted dunes. The clear freshwater is warmed by the sun.
Kayak or stand-up paddle on the Rio Aracatiaçu, from the mouth heading upstream. The route crosses zones of silent mangrove, sandbars, and stretches bordered by coconut palms leaning over the water.

visits.text: Genipabu dunes, 25 km north of Natal, form a massive shifting sand field in perpetual motion. Constant wind sculpts crests and valleys that change shape weekly—a miniature Sahara facing the Atlantic.

Forte dos Reis Magos (1598), a star-shaped fortress at the tip of a sand spit, guards the mouth of Rio Potengi. Built by the Portuguese then occupied by the Dutch, it recounts four centuries of colonial history.
Praia de Ponta Negra closes the circuit on an iconic note: Morro do Careca, a 120-meter dune covered with Atlantic vegetation, plunges directly into the ocean. This is Natal's postcard image.
Cross the Ponte Newton Navarro, a 56-meter-high cable-stayed bridge inaugurated in 2007. It spans Rio Potengi and offers spectacular views of Natal, the fort, and the northern coastline.

activities.text: Buggy tour through Genipabu dunes: the driver asks "com emoção ou sem emoção?" Those who choose "with emotion" hurtle down vertiginous sand slopes amid screams and laughter.
Swimming in Lagoa de Pitangui, a crystalline freshwater lagoon nestled between dunes and coconut palms. The warm, calm water contrasts with the nearby ocean waves.
Stroll along Praia de Ponta Negra at sunset. Morro do Careca blazes with orange hues while fishermen bring in their nets—the final act of a journey between dunes and ocean.

places.name: Nobres Bom Jardim

visits.text: Aquário Encantado is a 6-meter-deep basin fed by three springs from the Rio Salobra. The water, filtered through limestone, achieves total transparency. Over thirty fish species—red-finned piraputangas, golden dourados, cascudos—move between sand boils marking the springs. Touching the bottom is forbidden to preserve clarity.

Cachoeira Serra Azul, in the park managed by Sesc Pantanal, plunges 46 meters into a 30-meter azure basin populated with piraputangas. To reach it, count on 470 stairs down (and back up). A 700-meter zipline at 50 meters height offers a spectacular alternative to the staircase on the way back.

Refúgio das Águas, a few minutes from Aquário Encantado, conceals two natural pools fed by underground springs in the heart of gallery forest. The water is absolutely clear; the cool temperature contrasts with the cerrado heat.

Lagoa das Araras, 1 km from Bom Jardim's center, is a wetland bordered by buriti palms whose hollow trunks serve as nests. Every evening around 5pm, hundreds of blue-and-yellow macaws, maritacas, and parakeets converge on the lagoon in a spectacular revoada.

activities.text: Float down the Rio Salobra in flutuação (surface snorkeling) for 1,000 meters. The limestone gives the water transparency that reveals every detail of the bottom: schools of piraputangas, freshwater rays hidden in the sand, golden dourados. Life jacket and mask provided, guide mandatory.

Boia Cross at Duto do Quebó: descend 1,800 meters of river on an inflatable tube, alternating calm passages and rapids. The highlight: crossing a 330-meter-long cave, where your headlamp reveals stalactites, bats, and rock formations in total darkness.

places.name: Oeiras

visits.text: The Catedral de Nossa Senhora da Vitória, erected in 1733 on the site of a 1695 chapel, is the oldest religious building in Piauí. Its two asymmetrical towers — one topped with a bell, the other with a clock — frame a restrained baroque facade. The carved wooden altar blends rococo with sertão influences.

Oeiras, founded in 1712 as Vila da Mocha, served as the first capital of Piauí from 1759 to 1851. Its historic center, listed as a national monument, lines up colonial mansions with pastel facades, the Casa das Sete Janelas (House of Seven Windows) and the former governor's palace built in the 19th century.

The Museu de Arte Sacra of Oeiras preserves over 500 liturgical pieces from the town's three main churches: polychrome statues, silverwork, ex-votos and ornaments from the 18th and 19th centuries. Housed in the former town hall and jail, the building itself is worth the visit.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, built in 1836 by the Visconde de Parnaíba, overlooks the Largo do Rosário. Nearby, the Casa da Pólvora (Powder House), the first military building in Piauí and only the third of its kind in Brazil, is a rough-stone arsenal still standing.

activities.text: Climb Morro do Leme, a small hill topped by a monumental cross erected in 1945. From the summit, the panoramic view takes in the entire town of Oeiras, its bell towers and the semi-arid caatinga landscape of Piauí.

Try sertanejo cuisine in the restaurants downtown: carne de sol (sun-dried beef) grilled over charcoal, baião de dois (rice and beans), paçoca de carne pilé (pounded beef with manioc flour) and cajuína, a fermented cashew drink typical of Piauí.

places.name: Ouro Preto

visits.text: Praça Tiradentes, the heart of Ouro Preto, is dominated by the Museu da Inconfidência, housed in the former Town Hall and Prison of 1784. It tells the story of the 1789 independence movement led by Tiradentes and holds the remains of the conspirators in its underground Pantheon.

The Igreja de São Francisco de Assis, the absolute masterpiece of Aleijadinho — the mixed-race sculptor afflicted by leprosy — dazzles with its curving rococo facade, soapstone reliefs and a ceiling painted by Mestre Ataíde showing a mulatto Virgin surrounded by angel musicians.

The Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora do Pilar holds the most opulent interior in colonial Brazil: over 400 kilos of gold leaf cover the altars, columns and balconies. Red velvet curtains on the tribunes create a striking theatrical effect.

The Mina da Passagem, between Ouro Preto and Mariana, is the largest gold mine open to visitors in Brazil. A trolley descends 120 meters underground into 18th-century galleries. At the bottom, a translucent underground lake even allows swimming.
The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos, built by and for enslaved Africans, stands out with its unique rounded shape and curves that break from conventional baroque.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the steep cobblestone lanes of Ouro Preto. Every turn reveals a new perspective: twin bell towers framed by hills, colonial facades with wrought-iron balconies, panoramas of red-tile rooftops.

Dinner of comida mineira in a downtown restaurant: frango com quiabo (chicken with okra), feijão tropeiro (beans with manioc flour), tutu de feijão and pão de queijo, all paired with artisanal cachaça from Minas.

places.name: Paraty

visits.text: Paraty's historic center has been UNESCO-listed since 2019. Founded in 1667 as an export port for gold from Minas Gerais, it holds Brazil's largest concentration of Portuguese colonial architecture. Four 18th-century churches, whitewashed houses with colorful shutters, cobblestone streets that flood at high tide.
Igreja Santa Rita, built in 1722 by freed Blacks, is Paraty's oldest church. Its white baroque facade stands out against palms and mountains. It now houses the Museum of Sacred Art.

Igreja Nossa Senhora dos Remédios (1787), Paraty's main church, overlooks Praça da Matriz, the heart of colonial social life. Its sober facade, twin bell towers, and baroque interior with gilded altarpieces speak to the city's opulence during the gold era.

The historic center's streets are paved with polished schist that reflects light. The colonial grid layout, flood drainage channels, and absence of motor vehicles make Paraty one of the continent's best-preserved historic centers.

Paraty Bay counts over 50 islands and 100 beaches accessible only by sea. Schooner excursions last 5 hours and stop at deserted islands with turquoise waters, notably Piscina Natural Caixa d'Aço, a natural rock formation that creates a protected lagoon.

Trindade, a bohemian village 35 minutes by road, is protected by Serra da Bocaina Park. A single road accesses it. Praia Cepilho is renowned for surfing, Praia Cachadaço for its calm waters bordered by dense forest.

activities.text: Visit artisanal cachaça distilleries on foot or by bike from the center. Paraty was the cachaça capital in the 18th century; some copper stills still run on local sugarcane. The cinnamon and clove variety is the region's signature.

Kayak Jabaquara Bay in late afternoon: golden light on the islands and mangroves is particularly beautiful. Circle tropical islets, navigate mangrove corridors, reach deserted beaches accessible only by sea.
Dinner in the historic center at sunset. Tables spill onto cobblestone streets, candles glow in colonial windows. Fresh fish of the day, coconut moqueca, artisanal caipirinha.

Schooner excursion in the bay: early morning departure from the port, five hours among deserted islands. The captain anchors at snorkeling spots, where the seabed reveals turtles and tropical fish. Onboard: caipirinhas and fresh fruit.
Natural slide at Penha, 15 km from the center: a rock polished smooth by the current where you glide into a fresh-water pool deep in the Atlantic forest.
Hike in Serra da Bocaina from Paraty's heights: one of Brazil's best-preserved Atlantic forests. Trails lead to hidden waterfalls and offer ocean and island views in the distance.

places.name: Paraty Centro Historico

visits.text: Paraty's historic center has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2019. Founded in 1667 as the export port for Minas Gerais gold, it's the finest concentration of Portuguese colonial architecture in Brazil. Four 18th-century churches, whitewashed houses with colorful shutters, cobblestone streets that flood at high tide.
Igreja Santa Rita, built in 1722 by freed Africans, is Paraty's oldest church. Its white baroque façade stands out against the palms and mountains. Today it houses the Museum of Sacred Art.
Trindade, a bohemian village 35 minutes from Paraty, is protected by Serra da Bocaina Park. A single road leads in. Praia Cepilho is renowned for surf, Praia Cachadaço for calm, crystal-clear waters.

Paraty Bay harbors over 50 islands and 100 beaches accessible only by sea. Boat tours (escuna) last 5 hours and stop at deserted islands with turquoise waters: Ilha Comprida and the natural swimming pool Caixa d'Aço, a rock formation sculpted by nature.

The Penha Natural Waterslide, 20 minutes from the center, is a 15-meter smooth rock slide where river water propels you into a natural pool surrounded by Atlantic rainforest. Local fishermen adjust the water flow to control your speed.

activities.text: Visit artisanal cachaça distilleries on foot or by bike from the center. Paraty was Brazil's cachaça capital in the 18th century.

Wander the art galleries and cobbled lanes of the historic center in the late afternoon. Colonial houses shelter painting studios, artisan jewelers, and independent bookshops. At high tide, seawater floods the streets—a spectacle unique to Paraty.
Kayak around Jabaquara Bay: navigate tropical islands, glide through mangroves, reach deserted beaches at your own pace.

Brazil Itinerary: São Paulo, Paraty, Chapada dos Guimarães, Amazon & Rio

map_keywords: Atlantic Forest Serra da Bocaina Paraty

places.name: Parnaiba

visits.text: Porto das Barcas, a restored 19th-century warehouse district, lines up art galleries, craft shops and restaurants along the old wharf. This is where ships once loaded with carnaúba wax — the 'green gold' of Piauí — set sail for the world.

The historic center of Parnaíba preserves casarões (grand mansions) with pastel facades, witnesses to the prosperity of the river trade. The Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora da Graça, the Casa Simplício Dias and the Municipal Theater punctuate the walk.

The Delta do Parnaíba is the only delta in the Americas that empties into the open sea. The river splits into five arms forming over 80 islands across 2,700 km². Mangroves, dunes, lagoons and abundant wildlife: scarlet ibises, caimans and capuchin monkeys.
Lagoa do Portinho, 12 km from Parnaíba, is a freshwater lake surrounded by dunes and coconut palms. Its calm waters and fishing boats create a picture-postcard scene.

activities.text: Boat excursion through the Delta do Parnaíba: narrow igarapé channels wind between mangroves. At dusk, the revoada dos guarás sets the mangroves ablaze with red — hundreds of scarlet ibises converging on their roosting sites.
Seafood feast at Porto das Barcas: caranguejo (crab in sauce), grilled shrimp and peixada piauiense (local-style fish stew), all washed down with ice-cold cajuína.

places.name: Petropolis

visits.text: The Imperial Museum occupies Dom Pedro II's summer palace, built in 1845 in neoclassical style at the heart of the city he founded by decree in 1843. Polished parquet floors, music salons, Sèvres porcelain, and the imperial crown adorned with 639 diamonds are presented as they were in Empire times. The visit is conducted in slippers provided at the entrance.

Cathedral São Pedro de Alcântara is a neo-Gothic edifice completed in 1925. Its stained glass colors the nave with golden light. The tombs of Dom Pedro II, Empress Teresa Cristina, and Princess Isabel rest in the side chapel.

Palácio de Cristal is a glass-and-metal greenhouse prefabricated in France and assembled in Petrópolis in 1884. Princess Isabel organized exhibitions and high-society receptions here. Its transparent structure, with some panes tinted green and blue, now hosts exhibitions and concerts.

Avenida Koeler and Rua do Imperador concentrate the finest villas from the imperial era: neoclassical, neo-Gothic, Anglo-Norman, and Alpine chalets succeed each other along canals where the Quitandinha River flows. A Brazilian Paris lost in the mountains.

activities.text: Stroll through Petrópolis' historic center from the Imperial Museum to the Cathedral, passing Palácio Amarelo and the canals bordered by preserved Victorian villas.

Visit a craft brewery: Petrópolis, colonized by German immigrants in the 19th century, is Brazil's beer capital. The city's craft breweries produce lagers and ales following Germanic tradition, offering vat tours and tastings.
Sound and Light show at the Imperial Museum Thursday–Saturday evenings: the palace's pink facade lights up, projections evoke the imperial family's life and 19th-century Brazilian history.

map_keywords: Imperial Museum Palace Dom Pedro II Petrópolis

visits.text: The Chapadão dominates the coast from atop reddish cliffs eroded by wind and waves. This promontory offers panoramic views of the Atlantic, Praia do Amor below, and the rainforest carpeting the ravines.
Descend to Praia do Amor, whose bay forms a heart shape from the sky at low tide. Powerful waves make this Pipa's historic surf spot. Cliffs frame the beach like walls of a natural amphitheater.
Baía dos Golfinhos (Dolphin Bay) hides at the foot of vegetation-covered cliffs. Accessible at low tide by beach or by boat, it shelters a permanent dolphin colony that leaps just meters from shore.

Santuário Ecológico de Pipa protects a fragment of semi-arid Atlantic forest. Its 16 trails traverse cliffs, dunes, and viewpoints where you'll spot marmosets, exotic birds, and sometimes sea turtles during nesting season.

Praia do Madeiro unfolds its golden sand at the foot of cliffs draped in tropical forest. A sea turtle preservation zone, this beach has been ranked among the world's most beautiful by international travelers.

activities.text: Morning surf at Praia do Amor: consistent waves and Nordeste swell provide steady conditions. Rent a board on site and glide facing the cliffs—a setting that exists nowhere else.
Speedboat excursion to Baía dos Golfinhos and Guaraíras lagoon. Dolphins accompany the boat; the lagoon opens onto mangrove landscapes gilded by evening light.
Hike through the ecological sanctuary at sunset. Trails emerge onto vertiginous viewpoints above the ocean—with luck, a pod of dolphins plays in the waves below.
Evening stroll through Pipa's lively center. Cobbled streets overflow with seafood restaurants, cocktail bars, and live music—the cosmopolitan vibe of the Nordeste's most festive village.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Brazil Road Trip: Beaches and Dunes from Jericoacoara to Natal

visits.text: Pirenópolis, founded in 1727 by gold prospectors, is one of the oldest towns in Goiás. Its cobblestone streets, low colonial houses and three 18th-century churches form a historic center listed as a national heritage site. The Rio das Almas runs through town, its banks lined with restaurants and bars in restored buildings.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, built in 1732, is the oldest church in Pirenópolis. Destroyed by fire in 2002, it was faithfully reconstructed and now houses the Museu da Matriz, which traces the restoration process and the town's sacred heritage.

The Museu das Cavalhadas displays the spectacular masks and embroidered costumes worn during the Cavalhadas de Pirenópolis, a horseback pageant reenacting the battle between Moors and Christians. Held every year at Pentecost since 1826, it is one of Brazil's grandest folk festivals.

The Cachoeira do Abade, 16 km from the center along a partly unpaved road, tumbles down a sandstone amphitheater deep in the cerrado forest. A hanging bridge and a panoramic trail let you approach the main cascade and its crystal-clear pools.

activities.text: Hike in the Parque Estadual dos Pireneus: the trail climbs to Pico dos Pireneus at 1,385 m, the highest point in the region. The view stretches across endless cerrado, reddish hills echoing the mining past and, on a clear day, the plains of Goiânia on the horizon.

Stroll along Rua do Lazer, the pedestrian street that comes alive in the evening. Restaurants serve galinhada goiana (chicken and rice with pequi), pamonha (corn paste steamed in its husk) and empada goiana, washed down with artisanal cachaça from the area.

Cool off at Cachoeira do Coqueiro or the Vargem Grande reserve, where the Santa Maria and Lázaro waterfalls tumble through regenerating cerrado. The cold natural pools offer welcome relief from the plateau heat.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

places.name: Porto De Galinhas

visits.text: Porto de Galinhas—a former fishing village 70 kilometers south of Recife—owes its name to the era of slave trade: "There are new hens at the port" signaled the clandestine arrival of enslaved people. Today its low-rise streets are lined with seafood restaurants, craft shops, and galleries of painted rooster sculptures—a knowing nod to this dark history.

The natural pools of Porto de Galinhas form at low tide among coral reefs offshore. Traditional jangada sailboats ferry swimmers ten minutes to turquoise basins where tropical fish, starfish, and sea urchins drift through less than a meter of crystalline water. The "Mapa do Brasil"—a coral formation shaped like the country—is the iconic photo spot.

Pontal de Maracaípe, at the mouth of the Maracaípe River, shelters a mangrove ecosystem where seahorses reproduce. Jangada fishermen dive to show the tiny cavalos-marinhos in a jar before releasing them. Sunset from the pontal—with coconut palms and mangroves silhouetted against golden light—is the region's finest.

Projeto Hippocampus, in the village center, is a research facility dedicated to seahorse reproduction and conservation. Observation aquariums and educational displays explain the life cycle of these threatened creatures.

Praia de Muro Alto, to the north, boasts one of the region's largest natural pools: a coral wall traps water at low tide across more than a kilometer. Calm shallow water, white sand, and swaying palms—ideal for families.

activities.text: Walk barefoot along Porto de Galinhas beach at sunset. Multicolored jangadas are pulled up on the sand, beachside kiosks serve passion fruit caipirinhas, and the Atlantic stretches unbroken to Africa.

Buggy tour from beach to beach: Cupe for surfing, Maracaípe for waves and seahorses, Muro Alto for the expansive natural pools. Each stop reveals a different coastal landscape of reefs, dunes, and coconut groves.

Walk along the beach from Porto de Galinhas to Cupe, then onward to Muro Alto (8 kilometers one way). At low tide, Cupe's natural pools offer peaceful snorkeling away from the crowds near the village center.

places.name: Porto Seguro

visits.text: The Centro Histórico of Porto Seguro, perched on a cliff, is the exact spot where the Portuguese landed in 1500. The Marco do Descobrimento, a stone column brought from Portugal, and the Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Penha (1535) overlook the ocean and the reefs.

Arraial d'Ajuda, across the river by ferry, is a bohemian village of colorful lanes, art galleries and colonial churches. Praia de Mucugê, backed by multicolored cliffs, is one of the most photographed beaches in Bahia.

Praia do Espelho, 25 km south of Arraial, ranks among the most beautiful beaches in Brazil. At low tide, natural pools between the reefs reflect the sky like a mirror — hence the name. White cliffs and turquoise water complete the picture.

Trancoso, a former Jesuit village founded in 1583, centers on the Quadrado, a rectangular grass square lined with colorful houses and the Igreja de São João Batista, one of the oldest churches in Brazil. The far end of the Quadrado opens onto a cliff overlooking the ocean.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling in the natural pools of Praia do Espelho or Coroa Vermelha. The coral reefs shelter tropical fish, starfish and seahorses.
Sunset from the Quadrado in Trancoso: the sky turns crimson above the cliff as bars set up tables on the grass. A passion-fruit caipirinha is non-negotiable.

places.name: Recife

visits.text: Recife Antigo, the port district on the island of the same name, centers on the Praça do Marco Zero, starting point of the city founded by the Dutch. Rua do Bom Jesus is home to the Synagogue Kahal Zur Israel, the oldest in the Americas (1636), and restored warehouses turned galleries.

Olinda, 7 km from Recife, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982. Founded by the Portuguese in 1535, burned by the Dutch in 1631 and then rebuilt, it lines up twenty baroque churches, convents and pastel-colored colonial houses across eight hills facing the Atlantic.

The Convento de São Francisco in Olinda, founded in the 16th century, preserves 16 panels of Portuguese azulejo tiles depicting the life of Saint Francis. The cloister, gilded woodwork and painted sacristy are worth the visit alone.

The Alto da Sé, the highest point in Olinda, brings together the Catedral da Sé, tapioca stalls and a vertiginous viewpoint. From here, the view sweeps across tile rooftops, bell towers, a fringe of coconut palms and, in the distance, the skyscrapers of Recife.

activities.text: Walk the narrow streets of Olinda: artists' studios open their doors to galleries of naïve paintings, wood sculptures and carnival masks. Rua do Amparo has the best addresses.
Enjoy a stuffed tapioca at sunset from the Alto da Sé, paired with a cajá-fruit caipirinha. Street vendors set up their grills facing the panorama.
Swim at Boa Viagem, a 7 km urban beach lined with coconut palms and protected by a reef barrier. Swim only in signed areas.

places.name: Rio De Janeiro Leme

visits.text: Corcovado peaks at 710 m. Christ the Redeemer, unveiled in 1931, stands 28 m tall with arms outstretched. From the summit platform: Copacabana, Ipanema, Guanabara Bay, Sugarloaf and Tijuca Forest spread out in a full 360° sweep.
Sugarloaf (396 m) is reached by two cable cars from Praia Vermelha. The view takes in the entire city, and is especially stunning in the late afternoon when golden light floods the bay.
The Escadaria Selarón is a stairway of 215 steps covered with over 2,000 ceramic tiles collected from around the world. It links the Lapa neighborhood to Santa Teresa.
Santa Teresa is Rio's Montmartre: steep cobblestone lanes, colorful colonial houses and artists' studios.

Tijuca Forest covers 33 km² — the largest urban tropical forest in the world. Ocelots, monkeys and 300 bird species. Parque Lage at its entrance combines gardens, waterfalls, a 19th-century mansion and a contemporary art gallery.
The Cathedral of São Sebastião, built between 1964 and 1979, is a 96-meter-tall concrete cone 104 meters in diameter. Four giant stained-glass panels run from floor to ceiling.

The Tijuca National Park shelters 33 km² of dense tropical rainforest within the city limits — the world's largest urban jungle. Trails lead to the Mesa do Imperador lookout and the Cascatinha Taunay waterfall, a 30-meter cascade tucked into the canopy. At the park's entrance, Parque Lage pairs a stately 19th-century mansion with lush gardens and a contemporary art school.

São Sebastião Cathedral rises in the city center like a massive concrete cone, 96 meters tall and 104 meters across. Inside, four floor-to-ceiling stained-glass panels pour colored light into the cavernous nave, creating an almost otherworldly atmosphere.

The Museu do Amanhã (Museum of Tomorrow), opened in 2015 on the revamped Praça Mauá waterfront, is an architectural feat by Santiago Calatrava. Its interactive exhibitions explore humanity's major environmental and climate challenges.

Praça XV and its surroundings form the colonial heart of Rio. The Paço Imperial (1743), the former royal residence, the Igreja Nossa Senhora do Carmo and the lanes of the old Portuguese town tell the story of Rio before the beaches.

Rio's Jardim Botânico, founded in 1808 by Dom João VI, spreads across 54 hectares at the foot of Corcovado. Its imperial avenue of royal palms, planted in 1842, is one of the most famous in Brazil. Orchids, bromeliads, Victoria Amazonica water lilies and a butterfly greenhouse.

Ipanema and Leblon, Rio's two chicest beaches, stretch 4 km along the Atlantic. Numbered kiosks (postos) structure beach life: each posto has its own vibe, its crowd, and its vendors selling mate and açaí.

The Museu Nacional de Belas Artes, founded in 1937 in a neoclassical palace downtown, holds the largest collection of 19th-century Brazilian painting as well as European works brought over by the royal court in 1808.

activities.text: Early-morning walk along the Copacabana mosaic promenade (4.5 km) to Ipanema: jogging, beach volleyball, surfing. Leave valuables at the hotel.
Hike from Parque Lage to Corcovado: 3 hours through Tijuca's tropical jungle, +700 m elevation gain.
Sunset cruise on Guanabara Bay: Sugarloaf and Corcovado seen from the water.
Head to Arpoador, between Copacabana and Ipanema, 30 minutes before sunset. The cariocas applaud as the sun disappears.
Early-morning walk along the Copacabana mosaic promenade (4.5 km) to Ipanema: jogging, beach volleyball, surfing. Leave valuables at the hotel.
Head to Arpoador, between Copacabana and Ipanema, 30 minutes before sunset. The cariocas applaud as the sun disappears.
Visit the Niterói Museum of Contemporary Art (MAC) across the bay: Oscar Niemeyer's white flying-saucer perched on a cliff, with a panoramic view back to Rio. Reach it by ferry from downtown.
Saturday feijoada lunch in Santa Teresa or the Lapa district: a slow-cooked stew of black beans and smoked meats, caipirinhas and live samba. The quintessential carioca weekend ritual.
Ipanema Hippie Fair (Praça General Osório) on Sunday morning: crafts, jewelry, paintings and semi-precious stones. The largest street market in Rio.

Hike Dois Irmãos from the Vidigal favela: 45 minutes up to the twin peaks that tower over Ipanema. A 360° view of the coastline, the lagoons, Christ the Redeemer and the bay. The trailhead is at the top of Vidigal, reached by communal moto-taxi.

places.name: Salvador

visits.text: The Pelourinho, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, unfolds its brightly colored colonial houses across the hills of the Cidade Alta. Founded in the 16th century, this district was the first slave market in the Americas. Today its cobblestone lanes pulse with drums and Afro-Bahian music.

The Igreja e Convento de São Francisco, completed in 1723, is known as 'the golden church': over 800 kilos of gold leaf cover the altars, columns and carved ceilings. The Portuguese azulejo panels in the cloister depict mythological scenes.

The Elevador Lacerda, an Art Deco public elevator from 1873, connects the Cidade Baixa to the Cidade Alta in 22 seconds across a 72-meter drop. At the bottom, the Mercado Modelo occupies the former customs house and sells crafts, berimbaus, essential oils — with live capoeira out front.

The Farol da Barra, a lighthouse built in 1698 on the site of Salvador's first fort, commands the headland where the Baía de Todos os Santos meets the Atlantic. The nautical museum inside traces the maritime history of Bahia.

activities.text: Watch a roda de capoeira on the Terreiro de Jesus, the central square of the Pelourinho. Masters (mestres) play the berimbau while capoeiristas flow through acrobatics and dodges inside the circle.
On Tuesday evenings, the Pelourinho comes alive with blocos of Afro-Bahian music: the Olodum drums thunder through the narrow streets, replaying the rhythms that have inspired artists worldwide.

Try acarajé, a black-eyed pea fritter deep-fried in palm oil and stuffed with vatapá (shrimp cream), caruru and hot pepper. Bahian women in traditional dress prepare it on virtually every street corner.

places.name: Sao Luis

visits.text: São Luís's Centro Histórico preserves over 3,500 buildings from the 17th, 18th, and 19th centuries—the largest collection of Portuguese colonial architecture in Latin America. More than 300 different azulejo tile patterns dress the facades, imported from Portugal starting in the 1840s to protect walls from tropical humidity. The district has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1997.

Walk up Rua Portugal, where you'll find the city's highest concentration of azulejo-clad facades. Stone pavement leads to Beco Catarina Mina, a narrow alley flanked by sobrados with wrought-iron balconies. A museum dedicated to azulejo tiles occupies the ground floor of a restored townhouse.

The Palácio dos Leões, seat of the Maranhão state government, has overlooked the Bay of São Marcos since the 17th century. Behind its neoclassical facade lie reception rooms furnished with period pieces and paintings. The neighboring Igreja da Sé cathedral preserves gilded altars and a restored pipe organ.

The Teatro Arthur Azevedo, inaugurated in the 19th century, is one of Brazil's oldest theaters still in operation. Its Italian-style hall seats 800 beneath a painted ceiling. Guided tours reveal the original stage machinery still in working condition.

activities.text: Stroll across the Praça Grande esplanade at sunset: the pastel facades turn golden while viewpoints offer sweeping vistas over the Bay of São Marcos and the historic center's terracotta rooftops.

São Luís is Brazil's reggae capital. Come evening, bars in the historic quarter pulse with reggae danced in pairs—a unique local tradition. Bumba-meu-boi, a folkloric spectacle mixing dance, music, and embroidered costumes, brings the squares to life during festival season.

Sample Maranhão cuisine in the alleyways of Reviver: cuxá (a sauce made with local herbs served over rice), grilled fish with tucupi, and juçara juice—the Amazonian cousin of açaí—sold by street vendors.
